coredata.dc:title: NiO@Carbon quantum dots S-scheme heterojunction as a light-activated oxidase mimic for Cu2+ colorimetric detection
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Shiyu
Han, Zhe
Yan, Guohao
Hou, Xinghui
Yi, Shasha
Zhang, Zongtao
Zhou, Ying
Zhang, Liying
coredata.dc:description: Light-activated oxidase mimics have a wide application prospect in environment, detection, medicine and other fields because of its stability, environmental friendliness and high activity. Here a S-scheme heterojunction formed by NiO nanosheets modified with carbon quantum dots (CQDs) is prepared as a light-activated oxidase mimic. The modification of CQDs expands the light absorption range of NiO, while NiO as a matrix improves the dispersion of CQDs. Moreover, at the contact interface a S-scheme heterojunction forms, accelerating the separation of photoexcited electrons and holes under the built-in electric field and band bending. The unique transfer of photoexcited carriers maintains high oxidation and reduction activity of NiO@CQDs, improving its photocatalytic activity. NiO@CQDs can oxidize o-phenylenediamine (OPD) to yellow under light, and when Cu2+ was present in the solution, this colorimetric reaction was inhibited. Therefore, NiO@CQDs could be used for colorimetric detection of Cu2+, with a wide linear range of detection (0–0.1 µM, 0.1–1 µM, 1–10 µM, 10–100 µM), low detection limit (0.024 μM), good selectivity and stability.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Light-activated oxidase mimic
Carbon quantum dots
Colorimetric detection
S-scheme heterojunction
Nanozyme

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of NiO sheets and CQDs
Selectivity, stability of the method and Cu2+ detection in actual water samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22002142). Thanks for the EPR instrument support provided by Modern Analysis and Gene Sequencing Center of Zhengzhou University

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD spectrum (a), SEM image (b), TEM image (c) and HRTEM image (d) of NiO@CQDs-3; element mapping images of NiO@CQDs-3 (e-h); high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1 s (i), Ni 2p (j) and O 1 s (k).
UV − vis spectra (a), band gap energies (b), PL spectra at an excitation wavelength of 285 nm (c) and photocurrent responses (d) of samples with different CQDs modification ratios.

UV–Vis spectra of NiO@CQDs-3 + OPD + Light, NiO + OPD + Light, CQDs + OPD + Light, NiO@CQDs-3 + OPD + dark (a); UV–Vis spectra of NiO, NiO@CQDs-1, NiO@CQDs-2, NiO@CQDs-3, NiO@CQDs-4 with OPD under illumination, respectively (b); UV − vis spectra and digital photos of NiO@CQDs-3 + OPD + light with varying reaction time (c), OPD concentrations (d), pH (e) and temperature (f). Experiments were carried out using 15 μg/mL samples in a 20 mL buffer.

Catalytic mechanism and the steady-state reaction rate of NiO@CQDs-3 (a) Michaelis − Menten plots; (b) Corresponding Lineweaver − Burk plots; (c) UV–vis spectra variation with different concentrations Cu2+ (inset: the corresponding photograph); (d) The linear calibration plot for Cu2+ detection derived from Fig. 4c. (A0 is the absorption of the blank solution, and A is the absorption of the testing solution; test condition: 15 μg/mL NiO@CQDs-3, 250 μM OPD, acetate buffer with pH = 4, 40 °C).
(a) Stability of NiO@CQDs-3 in 8 days; (b) absorption variation of NiO@CQDs-3 with addition of 1 µM Cu2+ and 100 µM other ions.

The energy band structures of NiO and CQDs before contact (a); the built-in electric field and band bending formed as NiO and CQDs got in contact with each other (b); the photoexcited h+ and e- transfer direction under the BIEF and band bending (c).

(a) UV − vis absorption spectra of different systems: NiO@CQDs-3 + OPD, NiO@CQDs-3 + OPD + BQ, NiO@CQDs-3 + OPD + EDTA, NiO@CQDs-3 + OPD + IPA (Inset was the photograph); (b) the corresponding bar chart of (a); (c) EPR test of h+; • O2 –; • OH and 1O2 -; XPS spectra of NiO@CQDs-3 after reacting with Cu2+:(d) full spectrum; (e) high-resolution spectrum of Ni 2p; (f) high-resolution spectrum of Cu 2p.
Determination results in real samples and recovery rate.

head.ce:title: NiO@Carbon quantum dots S-scheme heterojunction as a light-activated oxidase mimic for Cu2+ colorimetric detection

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Zhengzhou University, 100 Science Road, Zhengzhou 450001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Zhengzhou University, 100 Science Road, Zhengzhou 450001, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Shiyu Hu and Zhe Han contributed equally to this work and should be considered co-first authors.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Light-activated oxidase mimics have a wide application prospect in environment, detection, medicine and other fields because of its stability, environmental friendliness and high activity. Here a S-scheme heterojunction formed by NiO nanosheets modified with carbon quantum dots (CQDs) is prepared as a light-activated oxidase mimic. The modification of CQDs expands the light absorption range of NiO, while NiO as a matrix improves the dispersion of CQDs. Moreover, at the contact interface a S-scheme heterojunction forms, accelerating the separation of photoexcited electrons and holes under the built-in electric field and band bending. The unique transfer of photoexcited carriers maintains high oxidation and reduction activity of NiO@CQDs, improving its photocatalytic activity. NiO@CQDs can oxidize o-phenylenediamine (OPD) to yellow under light, and when Cu2+ was present in the solution, this colorimetric reaction was inhibited. Therefore, NiO@CQDs could be used for colorimetric detection of Cu2+, with a wide linear range of detection (0–0.1 µM, 0.1–1 µM, 1–10 µM, 10–100 µM), low detection limit (0.024 μM), good selectivity and stability.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Copper is an essential trace metal element in the human body that supports biocatalytic reactions, biological transport, and biosynthesis. At the same time, copper can trace some disease. For example, it has been found that the copper content in the serum and tumor tissues of various cancer patients has changed significantly. [1–3] Excessive copper intake can also lead to harmful effects such as inhibiting necessary enzyme activity, abnormal biological oxidation–reduction processes, and neurotoxicity. [4–5] Therefore, detection of copper ions is very important for cancer surveillance, food safety and environmental safety. The traditional method for detecting Cu2+ is generally based on spectroscopy, [6] which requires expensive instruments and complicated sample pretreatment. Colorimetric detection using nanozymes provides a sensitive and convenient alternative that has received significant attention. [7–8].

Nanozymes are a type of nanomaterial that can catalyze specific reactions. Compared to natural enzymes, they have higher stability and lower costs, making them suitable for various applications such as antibacterial, antioxidant, biological imaging, biological testing, and environmental testing. Some reported nanozymes include peroxidase mimics, [9] oxidase mimics, [10] laccase mimics [11] and superoxide dismutase mimics (SOD), [12] in which peroxidase mimics are widely used in colorimetric detection. However, the use of hydrogen peroxide in colorimetric detection using peroxidase mimics is harmful to the environment. [13] Alternatively, oxidase mimics use oxygen as a substrate and avoid the use of hydrogen peroxide, making them increasingly useful in colorimetric detection. [14–16].

Light-activated oxidase mimics are similar to oxidase mimics in that they use photoactive materials to generate electrons and holes under illumination, which further oxidizes O2 into superoxide radicals. The photogenerated electrons can be consumed by metal ions, so the addition of metal ions would affect the production of free radicals, subsequently affecting the colorimetric reaction. The concentration of the detected substance and the intensity of a specific absorption peak can be established to achieve the detection purpose. [17–18] Compared with oxidase mimics, light-activated oxidase mimic has superior activity and better affinity for chromogenic agents such as TMB, making it a promising agent for colorimetric detection. For instance, TiO2, ZnO, Ag3PO4, Carbon dots, light-active COF, etc. have all been reported for light-activated oxidase mimics. [19–23].

To further enhance the activity of light-activated oxidase mimics, it is generally effective to improve the photoactivity of semiconductors by constructing heterojunctions. And in the various heterojunctions, S-scheme heterojunction shows great advantages due to the built-in electric field at the interface, which accelerates the separation of photoexcited electrons and holes while exhibits strong photo-redox ability. [24].

Here a S-scheme heterojunction formed by NiO nanosheets and CQDs was prepared, which was then used as a light-activated oxidase mimic to colorimetric detect Cu2+. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) had full solar spectrum absorption, responding to both visible and near-infrared light, and lots of studies had shown that carbon dots have oxidase-like activity. [25–27] However, due to its small size, CQDs were easy to agglomerate in solution, which affected its activity, and the oxidase mimics activity of CQDs without metal doping was generally low. Nickel oxide (NiO) was a common photoactive material with a band gap of 3.7 eV, which could be excited by UV light.

The experimental results showed that the modification of CQDs expanded the light absorption range of NiO, while NiO as a matrix improved the dispersion of CQDs. Moreover, at the contact interface a S-scheme heterojunction formed, accelerating the separation of photoexcited electrons and holes under the built-in electric field and band bending. And the unique transfer of photoexcited carriers maintained high oxidation and reduction activity of NiO@CQDs, improving its photocatalytic activity. The synergistic effect increased the activity of NiO@CQDs as a light-activated oxidase mimic, and when it was used for the colorimetric detection of copper ions, it exhibited a wide detection range, high sensitivity and low detection limit, good stability and high selectivity, and can also be used for the detection of actual water sample.

Ethanol absolute (CH3CH2OH), isopropyl alcohol (IPA), p-Benzoquinone (BQ), copper chloride (CuCl2·2H2O), silver sulfate (AgSO4), barium chloride (BaCl2), calcium chloride (CaCl2), Cobalt chloride (CoCl2), Chromium chloride (CrCl3), sodium dichromate (Na2CrO4), iron chloride (FeCl3), nickel chloride (NiCl2), lead chloride (PbCl2), zinc chloride (ZnCl2), chloroplatinic acid (H2PtCl6) and chloroauric acid (HAuCl4) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Disodium hydrogen phosphate (Na2HPO4·H2O), sodium dihydrogen phosphate (NaH2PO4·2H2O), potassium chloride (KCl), and sodium ethylenediamine tetra acetic acid (EDTA) were purchased from Comere Chemical Reagents Co., Ltd. O-phenylenediamine (OPD), dimethylpyridine n-oxide (DMPO) and 2, 2, 6, 6-tetramethylpiperidine oxide (TEMPO) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. Ni(NO3)2, urea and K2S2O8 were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Platanus leaves were collected from the campus of Zhengzhou University. Dialysis strip (Viskase, MD: 44, MW: 3500) was purchased from Union Carbide Corporation. Filter membrane was purchased from Tianjin branch billion Lung Experimental equipment Co., Ltd. Lake water was taken from Meihu Lake in Zhengzhou University campus, and the supernatant was used after two days of subsidy.

The surface morphologies of the samples were investigated by scanning electron microscopy (SEM), which was produced by Zeiss Auriga. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained with X-ray diffraction (DX-2700BH, Dandong Haoyuan). The XRD data were recorded at 40 kV and 20 mA in the region of 20-80˚ with 0.02˚ step. Transmission electron microscope (TEM), Scanning transmission electron microscope (STEM) and High-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) was all obtained on a FEI Tecnai G2 F20. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Ultroviolet photoelectron spectrometer (UPS) was obtained on Escalab-250 (Thermo Fisher). The bias voltage used for CQDs and NiO was −2 eV and −5 eV, respectively. The photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra were measured with an excitation wavelength of 340 nm by FluoroMax-4. Ultraviolet–visible diffuse reflectance spectra (UV–Vis DRS) were determined by a UV–Vis spectro-photometer (Shimadzu UV-3600) equipped with an integrating sphere using BaSO4 as the reference sample. The photocatalytic performances of catalysts were evaluated via the supernatant solutions were analyzed with a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Shanghai Aoyi, UV-1600PC). Zeta potential was tested by a Zeta potentiometer (Shanghai aifeisi, FZT 500A).

The electrochemical response was evaluated in a three-electrode electrochemical workstation (Zhengzhou Seris RST5810F), the light was provided by a 300 W Xe lamp (PL-X3000, Beijing Prince Technology Co., Ltd.), while the light intensity at the photoanode surface is 180 mW cm−2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of NiO sheets and CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: NiO sheet was synthesized using a typical method. [28] 0.58 g Ni(NO3)2, 3 g urea and 0.1 g K2S2O8 were added to 40 mL deionized water, stirred for 30 min, and then transferred to 100 mL autoclave and treated at 90˚C for 90 min. After the reaction, the obtained black-gray powder was filtered, washed with ethanol and distilled water several times, and dried at 60˚C for 4 h in a vacuum drying oven, finally calcined in a muffle furnace at 300˚C for 1.5 h to obtain NiO powder. The synthesis of carbon quantum dots from plane tree leaves involves several steps. [29] Firstly, the leaves were washed and dried in an oven at 80˚C, then ground into powder and calcined in Ar at 600˚C for 6 h. Then, 0.2 g of the obtained black powder was added to 20 mL of deionized water and stirred evenly. The mixture was added to a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and maintained at 120˚C for 12 h. After the reaction, the yellow supernatant was collected and filtered through a 22 µm filter membrane. The solution was then subjected to dialysis using a strip with a molecular weight of 3500, changing the solution every 4 h for 24 h. Finally, the solution was freeze-dried for 8 h to obtain carbon quantum dots powder.

CQDs powder was dissolved in 20 mL deionized water to prepare 2.4 g/L CQDs solution. 0.2 g NiO powder was dissolved in 20 mL deionized water. Subsequently, different volumes of CQDs solution (100 µL, 200 µL, 300 µL, and 400 µL) were added to the NiO suspension, and the mixture was stirred for 1 h and the particles were collected. Then, the particles were washed several times with ethanol and distilled water and dried in a vacuum drying oven at 60 ˚C for 4 h. The resulting products were named NiO@CQDs-1, NiO@CQDs-2, NiO@CQDs-3, and NiO@CQDs-4.

10 mg NiO@CQDs powder and 50 µL 0.5 wt% nafion (Sigma, 5 wt%) solution were dispersed in 125 µL anhydrous ethanol and 325 µL distilled water. The above 50 µL suspension was dripped onto ITO and dried in a vacuum chamber. Then, the performance of NiO@CQDs was tested by a three-electrode system, with NiO@CQDs/ITO as the working electrode, graphite rod as the counter electrode, saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as the reference electrode and PBS solution as the electrolyte.

OPD solution and NiO@CQDs suspension were added to 20 mL 0.20 M HAc/NaAc buffer solution, and irradiated with 300 W Xe lamp for 40 min. During illumination, the suspension was stirred continuously, and the temperature was controlled by water bath. After illumination, the suspention was then collected and the ultraviolet visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectrum was measured at 300–800 nm.

To colorimetric detect Cu2+, 150 μL NiO@CQDs suspension (2 mg/mL) and 250 μL OPD solution (20 mM) were added to 20 mL 0.10 M HAc/NaAc buffer (pH = 4.0) with different concentrations of copper ions. The suspension was then irradiated with a 300 W Xe lamp for 40 min. Then, the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the suspension was measured, and the relationship between absorbance intensity (at 450 nm) and Cu2+ concentration was established.

The fabrication process of NiO@CQDs consisted of synthesis of NiO nanosheets by hydrothermal, preparation of CQDs by calcination, hydrothermal, and dialysis, and finally fabrication of NiO@CQDs by dipping NiO nanosheets in CQDs solution. XRD, SEM and TEM tests were used to observe the composition and morphology of NiO@CQDs. As shown in the XRD pattern of NiO@CQDs sample (Fig. 1
a), there were three characteristic peaks at 37.6°, 42.8° and 62.1°, which were correspond to the (111), (200) and (220) diffraction plane of NiO, respectively (JCPDS NO. 73–1519), [30] and the peak at 25.2° was assigned to the (020) plane of CQDs (JCPDS NO. 003–0401). [31] The XRD pattern indicated the fabrication of NiO@CQDs.

It could be seen from SEM image (Fig. 1 b) and TEM image (Fig. 1 c) that the NiO@CQDs was in the form of nanosheet. To investigate the inner crystal structure, part of the nanosheet (inside the circle of Fig. 1 c) was further magnified by HRTEM. In the HRTEM image in Fig. 1d, there was a very thin nanosheet with darked dots on it, from which lattice fringes with lattice spacing of 0.205 nm and 0.240 nm could be observed on the nanosheet, corresponding to the (200), (111) plane of NiO, respectively, [32] and lattice fringes with lattice spacing of 0.238 nm could be observed on the darker dots, corresponding to (020) plane of CQDs. [33] Moreover, the composition was confirmed by element mapping analysis, where Ni, O and C elements were evenly distributed on the whole NiO@CQDs (Fig. 1 e-h).

Moreover, the chemical state and elemental composition of NiO@CQDs were characterized by XPS. As shown in Fig. S2 , the full XPS survey spectrum showed that NiO@CQDs was made up of C, O, and Ni elements. Fig. 1 i displayed the high-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1 s, which could be deconvoluted into three peaks. Peaks located at 284.2 eV, 284.8 eV and 285.5 eV corresponded to C–C bond, C = O bond and C-O bond, indicating the presence of CQDs with functional groups. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of Ni 2p spectrum was presented in Fig. 1 j, in which the peaks fitted at 855.5 eV and 873.1 eV corresponded to 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 of Ni(II), respectively, while the peaks at 861.1 eV, 865.2 eV, and 879.1 eV were satellite peaks. Three peaks in the high-resolution XPS spectrum of O 1 s (Fig. 1 k) located at 528.0 eV, 530.3 eV and 2531.4 eV were attributed to C = O, Ni-O and C-O, respectively, demonstrating the presence of NiO and CQDs with functional groups. [37–39] Based on the above analysis, it could be verified that NiO@CQDs was successfully synthesized.

Proper modification of CQDs could enhance the light absorption range and reduce the recombination of photogenerated carriers, thus improving the photoactivity of NiO, but excessive modification of CQDs might lead to a shading effect, resulting in the decrease of the photoactivity of NiO. To determine the best modification ratio of CQDs, a series of NiO@CQDs samples with different CQDs ratios were prepared, and the photoactivity was tested by UV–Vis, PL, and photocurrent tests.

As shown in the UV–Vis absorption spectra of NiO@CQDs samples with different CQDs ratios (Fig. 2
a), pure NiO exhibited strong light absorption at around 300 nm, the introduction of CQDs not only improved the absorption, but also brought a redshift of the absorption edge, indicating the enlargement of the light absorption range, which was consistent with previous studies. [40] The absorption increased with the CQDs ratio at first and then decreased, and the NiO@CQDs-3 sample exhibited the highest absorption. Fig. 2 b showed the calculated band gap values of each sample based on the results of Fig. 2a, demonstrating that the addition of CQDs led to a decrease in the band gap of NiO. The band gap decreased with the CQDs ratio at first and then increased, and the NiO@CQDs-3 sample exhibited the lowest band gap (2.63 eV).

PL spectrum reflected the recombination efficiency of photoexcited electron-hole pairs in semiconductor materials, and the lower the PL intensity, the lower recombination rate. Compared with pure NiO, the PL intensity of NiO@CQDs decreased, indicating the decrease of the recombination of photoexcited electrons and holes (Fig. 2 c). The PL intensity also decreased with the CQDs ratio at first and then increased, and the NiO@CQDs-3 sample exhibited the lowest PL intensity. Furthermore, the photocurrent of the samples was recorded by the electrochemical workstation in 0.2 M PBS solution at of 0.6 V. As shown in Fig. 2 d, under the same testing conditions, the photocurrent increased with the modification of CQDs, and NiO@CQDs-3 sample exhibited the highest photocurrent.

Based on the above analysis, the NiO@CQDs-3 sample had the optimal CQDs modification ratio, displaying the best photoactivity with wide light absorption range and low recombination efficiency of photoexcited holes and electrons. [41].

Colorimetric reaction was carried out in the dark to analyze the oxidase-like activity of the samples. OPD was used as chromogenic agent, which could be oxidized to oxOPD (yellow) under illumination with an absorption peak at 450 nm. As shown in the inset of Fig.S2 , CQDs, NiO and NiO@CQDs-3 all showed oxidase-like activity as they could oxide the OPD to yellow in the dark without H2O2. In the UV–Vis spectra ( Fig. S3 ), the absorption peak of NiO@CQDs-3 was stronger than that of pure CQDs and NiO, indicating a higher oxidase-like activity of NiO@CQDs-3, which might be due to the improved dispersion. High Zeta potential meant weaker agglomeration trend and better dispersibility of the particles in the solution. Zeta potential of NiO@CQDs-3 (-37.03 mV) was higher than that of pure CQDs (-14.22 mV) and NiO (–32.19 mV), demonstrating that NiO@CQDs-3 had improved dispersibility, which meant more active reaction sites and better stability. [42–43].

In order to investigate the light-activated oxidase-like activity of the samples, colorimetric reaction was further carried out under illumination. As shown in Fig. 3
a, the UV–Vis absorption peak intensity at 450 nm was increased under illumination, indicating that the introduction of light obviously enhanced the oxidase-like activity of NiO@CQDs-3. In addition, NiO@CQDs-3 exhibited higher light-activated oxidase-like activity with higher peak intensity at 450 nm and more obvious color change than pure CQDs and NiO, which was consistent with the above-mentioned photoactivity.

As shown in the UV–Vis spectra of the colorimetric reaction systems with different NiO@CQDs samples ( Fig. 3 b), NiO@CQDs-3 exhibited the strongest peak intensity at 450 nm and the most obvious colorimetric reaction (the photograph inset), which was also consistent with the above-mentioned photoactivity. The subsequent tests were all carried out with NiO@CQDs-3 sample.

The optimal reaction condition of NiO@CQDs-3 as a light-activated oxidase mimic was further studied, including reaction time, pH value, OPD concentration and temperature. Fig. 3 c showed that the UV–Vis peak at 450 nm increased with reaction time, reached the maximum at 40 min, and then decreased with time, which might be due to the saturation of colorimetric reaction at 40 min, and oxOPD was easily reduced with further extension of time. Fig. 3 d reflected the relationship between the absorbance peak of the system and the OPD concentration, and the peak intensity reached the maximum when the OPD concentration was 250 μM. Similar to natural enzymes, the catalytic activity of NiO@CQDs-3 was also influenced by pH and temperature. As can be seen from Fig. 3 e, the optimal pH of the reaction was 4, and the oxidation of OPD would be inhibited if the pH was too high or too low. When the temperature was too low, the enzymatic reaction was slow, while when the temperature was too high, the superoxide radical produced in the reaction was unstable, which lead to the decrease of the enzymatic reaction activity. [44] Fig. 3 f showed that the optimum temperature of the reaction was 40 °C.

In order to further evaluate the light-activated oxidase-like activity of NiO@CQDs-3, the steady-state kinetic parameters were assessed according to the Michaelis-Menten equation. In the equation, [S], Vmax, Km and V represented concentration of the substrate, the maximum reaction velocity, Michaelis constant, and initial reaction velocity, respectively.
1 V
=
(
K m
V
m a x
)
(
1
[ S ]
)
+
(
1
V
m a x
)

As shown in Fig. 4
a, Michaelis-Menten curve was obtained by varying OPD concentrations, and the Vmax and Km values were calculated to be 2.27*10-8 M s−1 and 0.136 mM by the converted Lineweaver-Burk curve (Fig. 4 b). It could be seen from Table S1 that the Km value of NiO@CQDs-3 was much smaller than other reports about nanozyme using OPD as colorimetric agent, indicating that NiO@CQDs-3 had strong affinity to OPD and could effectively catalyze the oxidation of OPD.

The colorimetric detection performance of NiO@CQDs-3 for Cu2+ was further investigated under the optimal reaction condition (15 μg/ml NiO@CQDs-3 and 250 μM OPD in acetate buffer with pH 4.0, 40℃ and reaction time 40 min). To observe the change of colorimetric reaction, different concentrations of Cu2+ were added to the system under illumination. As shown in Fig. 4 c, with the increase of Cu2+ concentration, the colorimetric reaction became weaker (inset in Fig. 4 c), and the corresponding absorbance value at 450 nm became lower. After calculation (Fig. 4 d), it could be found that the change of Cu2+ concentration (0–––100 μM) had a linear relationship with the change of absorbance at 450 nm, and the linear equations were y = 6.005 + 0.005x (0–––0.1 μM), y = 0.702 + 0.580x (0.1–––1 µM), y = 0.100 + 1.182x (1–––10 µM) and y = 0.002 + 2.162x (10–––100 µM). The limit of detection (LOD) was 0.024 µM (S/N = 3, S was the standard deviation of absorbance of the blank sample and N was the sensitivity). The detection limit and linear range were superior compared to those of most other Cu2+ colorimetric methods ( Table S2 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity, stability of the method and Cu2+ detection in actual water samples
ce:section.ce:para:
In order to verify the practical application of the prepared sample, the stability and selectivity of NiO@CQDs-3 were tested. As shown in Fig. 5
a, the same NiO@CQDs-3 sample was used in colorimetric reaction for 8 consecutive days (using the optimal test condition, without Cu2+), the UV–Vis absorption peak intensity changed little in 8 days, and the photocurrent response and FTIR spectrum of the sample also changed little (Fig. S5), which indicated that the sample had good stability.

As many kinds of metal ions usually coexisted in the actual water environment, to eliminate the interference of other metal ions in the detection of Cu2+, selective test was also carried out with detection solutions containing 10 µM Cu2+ and 100 µM Ag+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Co2+, Cr3+, CrO4 2-, Fe3+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, PtCl6 2-, AuCl4 -, respectively. As shown in Fig. 5 b, with addition of the detection solutions, the absorption at 450 nm changed, and the change for Cu2+ was 10–20 times of that of other interfering ions, indicating that the NiO@CQDs had a good selectivity for colorimetric detection of Cu2+.

The band structure of CQDs was analyzed by PL and CV test. As shown in Fig. S6a, the band gap of CQDs was 2.32 eV, and in the CV curve of CQDs (Fig. S6b), there were two reduction peaks at −0.23 V and −0.82 V (vs. Ag/AgCl), thus the LUMO of CQDs was −0.63 V vs. NHE, and the HOMO position could be calculated to 1.69 V. [45–46] As shown in Fig. 6 , an S-scheme heterojunction was formed between NiO and CQDs when they got in contact. As calculated from UPS (Fig. S7a and Fig. S7b), the work functions of NiO and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were found to be 5.08 eV and 4.38 eV, respectively (Fig. 6 a). When in close contact, electrons would flow from CQDs with a higher Fermi level to NiO with a lower Fermi level, thus equalizing their Fermi levels. This redistribution of charge resulted in NiO becoming negatively charged and the CQDs positively charged. As a result, a built-in electric field was formed from the CQDs to NiO at the contact interface. Furthermore, due to the loss of electrons, upward band bending occurred in CQDs, and downward band bending occurred in NiO due to the gain of electrons (as shown in Fig. 6 b). As shown in Fig. 6 c, driven by the built-in electric field and energy band bending, photoexcited electrons on the CB of NiO migrated to the VB of CQDs, while photoexcited holes on the VB of CQDs migrated to the CB of NiO. However, the photoexcited electrons in the CB of CQDs and the photoexcited holes in the VB of NiO could be retained to a great extent, possessing strong reducing and oxidizing properties, respectively. [47] EPR tests (Fig. 7
c) showed that both • OH and • O2 – were present in the system under illumination, further verifying the formation of an S-type heterojunction.

To explore the possible reactive species involved in the colorimetric reaction, quenching experiments were carried out with EDTA, BQ and IPA, as h+, •O2 − and •OH scavenger, respectively. As illustrated in Fig. 7 a, b, when EDTA, BQ and IPA were added into the OPD + NiO@CQDs-3 mixture, the absorbance at 450 nm after illumination was significantly reduced, demonstrating that h+, •O2 − and •OH all played a vital role in the colorimetric reaction. The EPR test also confirmed the presence of h+, •O2 −, •OH and 1O2 − (Fig. 7 c).

Besides, NiO@CQDs-3 sample after the colorimetric reaction was collected, and the change of composition was tested by XPS. As shown in Fig. 7 d, the XPS full spectrum of NiO@CQDs-3 sample after colorimetric detection of Cu2+ showed a new peak belonging to Cu element compared with the sample before detection ( Fig. S2 ). In the high-resolution XPS spectrum of Ni 2p (Fig. 7 e), there were six fitting peaks, in which peaks at 861.1 eV, 865.2 eV and 879.1 eV were satellite peaks of Ni, peaks centered at 855.5 eV and 873.1 eV corresponded to 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 of Ni(II), respectively, and a new peak at 853.8 eV assigned to Ni (0) (compared with Fig. 1 j), which might be due to the reduction of Ni2+ in NiO by photoexcited electrons. And in the high-resolution XPS spectrum of Cu 2p (Fig. 7 f), a peak at 932.8 eV was attributed to 2p3/2 of Cu (II). [48] The results indicated that copper-containing compound was loaded on the surface of NiO@CQDs-3 during the colorimetric detection of Cu2+.

Based on the above analysis, the mechanism of colorimetric detection of Cu2+ with NiO@CQDs could be speculated. The dissolved oxygen, hydroxyl in water were redoxed by the photoexcited electrons and holes to form •O2 −, •OH and 1O2 −, which could oxidize OPD together with the photoexcited holes. When Cu2+ was added to the system, photoexcited electrons could reduce Cu2+ to metal Cu, while h+ •OH and •O2 − could then oxidize Cu to CuO, as the h+ (3.10 V), •OH (2.82 V), •O2 − (0.90 V) all owned a higher redox potential than Cu (-0.342 V). As Cu2+ consumed reactive species, the oxidation of OPD weakened. Therefore, the addition of Cu2+ could weaken the colorimetric reaction between NiO@CQDs and OPD. By establishing the relationship between the absorption peak intensity of oxOPD (at 450 nm) and the concentration of Cu2+, the concentration of Cu2+ could be detected.

As Cu2+ had empty d orbit, antibonding molecular orbital could be formed between Cu2+ and transition metal compound (TMC), thus reducing the surface energy of TMC [49–50]. Therefore Cu2+ had a high affinity to TMC and could be adsorbed on the surface of NiO by surface complexion/incorporation. In addition, hybrid orbit could be formed between Cu2+ and CQDs with rich surface functional groups, so Cu2+ could also be adsorbed on the CODs efficiently [51]. Moreover, due to the location of the energy band of NiO and CQDs, only a few parts of metal ions could be reduced by the photo-excited electrons [52], such as Cu2+, Ag+, PtCl6 2-, AuCl4 2-. In conclusion, CQDs@NiO exhibited specificity in both adsorption and reaction, so it showed good selectivity when used as a nanozyme for colorimetric detection of Cu2+.

In summary, CQDs modified NiO nanosheet-like structure was obtained by simple hydrothermal and impregnation treatment. NiO nanosheets matrix can effectively reduce the agglomeration of CQDs, and CQDs modification can improve the light absorption range of NiO and reduce the recombination of photoexcited electrons and holes. Moreover, at the contact interface a S-scheme heterojunction formed, accelerating the separation of photoexcited electrons and holes under the built-in electric field and band bending. And the unique transfer of photoexcited carriers maintained high oxidation and reduction activity of NiO@CQDs, improving its photocatalytic activity. The synergistic effect increased the activity of NiO@CQDs as a light-activated oxidase mimic, and when it was used for the colorimetric detection of copper ions, it exhibited a wide detection linear range (0–––0.1 µM, 0.1–––1 µM, 1–––10 µM, 10–––100 µM), a low detection limit (0.024 μM), good selectivity and stability, and can be used for actual water sample detection.

Shiyu Hu: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Zhe Han: Data curation, Formal analysis. Guohao Yan: Formal analysis. Xinghui Hou: Supervision. Shasha Yi: Supervision. Zongtao Zhang: Supervision. Ying Zhou: Resources, Supervision. Liying Zhang: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22002142). Thanks for the EPR instrument support provided by Modern Analysis and Gene Sequencing Center of Zhengzhou University

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2023.158206.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ion exchange conversion of Na-Birnessite to Mg-Buserite for enhanced and preferential Cu2+ removal via hybrid capacitive deionization

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Bao, J. Jin, M. Ma, F. Li. Ion exchange conversion of Na-Birnessite to Mg-Buserite for enhanced and preferential Cu2+ removal via hybrid capacitive deionization. ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces 2022, 14, 46646-46656. https://doi.org/10.1021/acsami.2c13086.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The role of bare and modified nano nickel oxide as efficient adsorbents for the removal of Cd2+, Cu2+, and Ni2+ from aqueous solution

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Mahdavi, N. Amini. The role of bare and modified nano nickel oxide as efficient adsorbents for the removal of Cd2+, Cu2+, and Ni2+ from aqueous solution. Environ. Earth Sci. 2016, 75, 1468-1483. https://doi.org/10.1007/s12665-016-6270-5.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Co/Fe co-doped ZIF-8 derived hierarchically porous composites as high-performance electrode materials for Cu2+ions capacitive deionization

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Wang, H. You, G. Wu, L. Huang, J. Yan, X. Liu, Y. Ma, M. Wu, Y. Zeng, J. Yu, H. Zhang. Co/Fe co-doped ZIF-8 derived hierarchically porous composites as high-performance electrode materials for Cu2+ions capacitive deionization. Chem. Eng. J. 2023, 40, 141621-141631. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2023.141621.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: mechanism, and applications of photodeposition in photocatalysis: A review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Wenderich, G. Mul. Methods, mechanism, and applications of photodeposition in photocatalysis: a review. Chem. Rev. 2016, 116, 23, 14587-14619. https://doi.org/10.1021/acs.chemrev.6b00327.
